title: Restore, revert, reset and reflog

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Restore, revert, reset and reflog; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Git Reference Documentation; current stable accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Restore, revert, reset and reflog
sources.claim: Safe recovery chooses an operation from the state to preserve: restore working changes, unstage the index, revert a published commit, or reset/reflog only with understood history consequences.

sources.locator: Pro Git; second edition online; section applicable to Restore, revert, reset and reflog
sources.claim: Safe recovery chooses an operation from the state to preserve: restore working changes, unstage the index, revert a published commit, or reset/reflog only with understood history consequences.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Safe recovery chooses an operation from the state to preserve: restore working changes, unstage the index, revert a published commit, or reset/reflog only with understood history consequences.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A bad commit already on main is undone with a new revert commit, preserving public history and reviewability. Your goal is to recover staged, committed and lost-ref mistakes in disposable repositories without overwriting shared history. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Issues, pull requests and review; Debugging and quality automation; Working tree, index and HEAD; Push and non-fast-forward rejection; Log, show and graph. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Safe recovery chooses an operation from the state to preserve: restore working changes, unstage the index, revert a published commit, or reset/reflog only with understood history consequences. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Uncommitted data may be unrecoverable, reflog is local and expires, reset modes differ, and rewriting shared history can discard collaborators' work. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Git undo is one universal command, and hard reset is the fastest safe way to reverse any mistake.

includes: Choose working-tree restoration, a new inverse commit, local ref movement or reflog recovery based on publication state.
Recover staged, committed and lost-ref mistakes in disposable repositories without overwriting shared history.
excludes: Uncommitted data may be unrecoverable, reflog is local and expires, reset modes differ, and rewriting shared history can discard collaborators' work.

outcomes: Recover staged, committed and lost-ref mistakes in disposable repositories without overwriting shared history.

examples.title: Restore, revert, reset and reflog: direct case
examples.situation: A bad commit already on main is undone with a new revert commit, preserving public history and reviewability.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A bad commit already on main is undone with a new revert commit, preserving public history and reviewability.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Inspect working tree, index, commits, remotes, and reflog first; state what must survive, then choose the least destructive operation that changes only the intended layer.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Inspect working tree, index, commits, remotes, and reflog first; state what must survive, then choose the least destructive operation that changes only the intended layer.

examples.title: Restore, revert, reset and reflog: boundary case
examples.situation: A staged file is unstaged without discarding its working-tree edit because the learner distinguishes index recovery from content deletion.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A staged file is unstaged without discarding its working-tree edit because the learner distinguishes index recovery from content deletion.
Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Uncommitted data may be unrecoverable, reflog is local and expires, reset modes differ, and rewriting shared history can discard collaborators' work.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Git undo is one universal command, and hard reset is the fastest safe way to reverse any mistake.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Recover staged, committed and lost-ref mistakes in disposable repositories without overwriting shared history.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Git undo is one universal command, and hard reset is the fastest safe way to reverse any mistake.
misconceptions.correction: Inspect working tree, index, commits, remotes, and reflog first; state what must survive, then choose the least destructive operation that changes only the intended layer.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Git undo is one universal command, and hard reset is the fastest safe way to reverse any mistake.

analogy.example: A secured checkpoint system can model Restore, revert, reset and reflog: each revision is inspected, credentials are restricted, incidents revoke access, and recovery preserves known-good state.
usefulFor: Connecting pipeline evidence, secret response, reversible change, and collaborative policy
limitations: CI isolation, credential propagation, Git reachability, and distributed copies require technical controls beyond checkpoint paperwork.

recap.summary: You should now be able to recover staged, committed and lost-ref mistakes in disposable repositories without overwriting shared history. Keep this boundary in view: Uncommitted data may be unrecoverable, reflog is local and expires, reset modes differ, and rewriting shared history can discard collaborators' work.
